I think you haven't correctly unsderstood my problem.
In our team we use buildout to develop, build and distribute packages.
Buildout use egg format of python packages. 
In case of egg format expand_resource_path returns paths link '/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/sumy-0.4.1a0-py3.5.egg/sumy/data/stopwords/english.txt' and it's not a legal file (because of .egg is zip-archive).

For this cases pkgutil ( https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/pkgutil.html#pkgutil.get_data )is single known for me standard way to get access to distributed package data.
Of course I can explicitely add possibility to read in-archive pathes for reading data into your code (it you will insist on it) - but I think that this is usage of standard pkgutil is more pretty way to solve problem.

@jonparrott , for batching, wouldn't we just throw our methods into a context manager that reconciles all this stuff?

For example, here's what I envision as the code I'd write to do a bunch of detecting:

```python
image1 = client.image('/path/to/image1.jpg')
image2 = client.image('/path/to/image2.jpg')

faces = image1.detect_faces(limit=10)
labels = image1.detect_labels()
other_image_has_face = image2.has_faces()

# Now you have all the stuff you want, but you made 3 different API calls for it.
```

You then realize that this is too slow, and you want to batch this up into a single API call...

```python
image1 = client.image('/path/to/image1.jpg')
image2 = client.image('/path/to/image2.jpg')

with client.batch():
  faces = image1.detect_faces(limit=10)
  labels = image1.detect_labels()
  other_image_has_face = image2.has_faces()

# Now you have all the stuff you want, and we bulked it into a single request.
```

```python
def run(self):

    stop_flag = self.stop_flag
    interval = self.interval
    try:

Currently, the UI waits until a server response to handle either success or failure. This leaves open the possibility that the UI might feel unresponsive on a slow connection, or even produce an error if the user hits the toggle switch twice. (You can demo this by turning on network throttling in chrome tools.)

Simple solution: disable the toggle switch while the response is pending

More complex: optimistically update vuex and the UI with the intended new value immediately on-click. A success becomes a no-op, and an error would probably roll back to the previous value.

Another thing to watch out for is if the user manages to navigate away from the page before the response comes back. In other actions, we've tried to leverage the `ConditionalPromise` to handle this case. The idea is that success or failure handlers are only called based on a certain condition, typically that the user is on the same same page. (See `coreActions.samePageCheckGenerator`)

We skip certificate verification here since we do not have root certs installed in alpine linux, and can therefore not verify certs issued by public root CAs. I think this is acceptable because we are in control of the endpoint, and since this would presumably be run in a non-hostile network, the chances of a MITM attack are low. The alternative here is to install root certs on the base image, or let the user specify a pinned cert. Specifying a cert to pin would be preferable from a security standpoint, although since the cert of a log aggregator may be out of the user's control, and could be changed at any time, it is somewhat brittle.

I think this method is overcomplicated. Why not do: 

```python
def while_not_at_setpoint(tolerance, settling_time): 
    
    t = time.time()
    t0 = t
    
    while t - t0 < settling_time: 
        
        t = time.time()
        if abs(temperature() - setpoint()) > tolerance: 
            t0 = t
        
        time.sleep(0.1)
```
